title: Home
text:
Conservation agronomist | M.S. Biology student | Wildlife disease ecology | One Health
I am a conservation agronomist and master’s student in Biology at the University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez, interested in wildlife disease ecology, conservation physiology, One Health, and the movement of pathogens across human-modified landscapes.
My current work focuses on Toxoplasma gondii at the land-sea interface in Puerto Rico, with emphasis on environmental transmission pathways and conservation implications for the endangered Antillean manatee.

title: About
text:
About
I am a conservation agronomist and master’s student in Biology at the University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez. My academic background combines Animal Science, Natural Resources Conservation and Development, and Environmental Sociology and Public Policy, giving me a multidisciplinary foundation in animal biology, conservation, environmental systems, and the social dimensions of natural resource management.
My research interests focus on wildlife health, conservation physiology, One Health, comparative anatomy, reproductive biology, and the effects of landscape change, anthropogenic activity, and climate change on threatened species. My current graduate research examines Toxoplasma gondii at the land–sea interface in Puerto Rico, with emphasis on pathogen movement from terrestrial environments into coastal ecosystems and its potential implications for the endangered Caribbean manatee. Through this work, I integrate disease ecology, physiology, wildlife conservation, environmental health, molecular diagnostics, bioinformatics, and community-based outreach. I am particularly interested in using veterinary medicine and biological sciences together to understand how disease, environmental stressors, reproductive processes, tissue-level changes, and physiological responses shape the health and survival of wildlife populations. This interest connects my current work in ecological disease and conservation with my broader goal of applying comparative anatomy, embryology, reproductive biology, and conservation physiology to the study and protection of endangered species.
I am deeply committed to community-based science because I believe communities have the power to reclaim, care for, and develop a sense of belonging within the spaces they inhabit, and that they are essential partners in putting conservation into action. I bring experience in conservation, animal science, research support, scientific writing, project coordination, environmental education, and community outreach. My long-term goal is to pursue combined DVM/PhD training and contribute to wildlife medicine, conservation research, reproductive and physiological approaches to species preservation, and the protection of endangered species worldwide.
More content coming soon.

title: Projects
text:
Research on Toxoplasma gondii transmission across terrestrial, estuarine, and marine environments in Puerto Rico, with conservation relevance for the endangered Antillean manatee.



A retrospective analysis of human diagnostic records to characterize evidence of T. gondii exposure in Puerto Rico.



A tissue and cellular biology project using zebrafish (Danio rerio) ZF4 cells infected with Toxoplasma gondii.



Science communication and community-based conservation outreach.
